examples.prompt: Write about a recent retreat or getaway where you were able to focus on writing or another creative pursuit.
examples.reference:
•5:00-7:00 p.m. Fresh air, walking, and creative well refilling. Whether that was strolling through an art gallery, walking around looking at historic buildings, churches, and sculptures - it was a brain break.

•7:00-8:30 p.m. Dinner. Naturally, we'd end up talking about our work, which led to life, family, and all the things business friends talk about.

•9:00-10:00 p.m. Relax, unwind, then bed.

I didn't have a word count goal going into the retreat, but I had about six ideas bulleted out that I needed to flesh out. I guessed that each of the six ideas would be roughly the length of a blog post. It turns out I was right.

•After day one, hour one, I had written 3,557 words (which counted the bullets I had outlined ahead of time). My most productive session!

•By the end of day one, I had 4,700 words.

•By 10:00 a.m. on day two, I had 5,680 words. And I spent much of the second day revisiting what I wrote on day one, which meant tweaking and adding stuff.

•By 11:00 a.m. (lunchtime), I had 6,231 words.

•On day three, I had a three-hour writing marathon in the middle of the day for my community (which meant they were writing and I was coaching) and had to scoot out early to get to my son's playoff hockey game, so I didn't write as much as I thought I would.

•Total three-day writing time for my business: eleven hours.

•Total new words for my business: 8,200 words.

When I first saw the total word count and time invested for three days, my ego wanted to feel crappy about it - I should have prepared more, pushed harder, and written more.

But I stopped that mind chatter. I did more than enough. At this time, I wasn't yet aligning my writing activities with nature's rhythms. We had some great conversations, and I had to think through some business decisions as these came up in my writing. And there were 8,200 words to use in my business that didn't exist before.

Here's an estimated breakdown of my total costs:

•Airbnb for three nights (my portion): $260

•Gas: $30

•Parking: $100

•Lunches and dinners: $400 (this was the highest because I like food and picked up the tab for at least one dinner)

Total cost: $790.

You could do it for much more or much less. Travel costs were low, and food costs were relatively higher.

My biggest worry going into this writing retreat: If I was investing all this time and energy in this trip, I wanted to be sure I'd get more writing done than I would have at home.

Since I didn't plan much in advance, I worried that I'd have too much client work to do or that I wouldn't feel inspired, clear, or even have the stamina to write anything useful. Next time, I'll plan it further in advance and make sure to block my calendar, so I have complete focus.

One of us didn't write for the specific project we were working on at all on day one - and that's okay.

After our second Pomodoro sprint, as I was pouring a third cup of coffee (the cups were tiny), one friend looked up from her laptop and said, "I'm not writing what I'm supposed to."

Without missing a beat, I replied, "That's okay." Because it is okay. She was writing something - that's the important part. She was journaling, emailing, and doing other writing.

It was so powerful to see living proof that my belief that writer's block is BS was accurate. She had some stuff to sort out in her mind and heart before she could get to her intended project.

By the afternoon, she was ready to work on her project, and by day two, she wrote like an intuitive MOFO.

I hope this inspires you to create a little writing retreat of your own - to start your website, a book, a screenplay, a few months of blog content, a bunch of poems, or whatever it is you want to write.

Extended vacations can turn into a writing retreat. I've kick-started lots of projects on planes sans Wi-Fi. You'll love how Mairéad, a writing community member, turned travel into a daily writing habit.

Before 2020, we used to travel for months from Ireland to mainland Europe in our camper van. I wrote a blog post most days. I cannot understand where I found the time. Travel in a van has lots of extra chores because it's not connected to water or electricity, it has no dishwasher or washing machine, and on damp days there's no suitable place to hang your wet clothes. It can get very uncomfortable.

fine_tuned.response:
It was like 3:00 am and I woke up in my hotel room with my bladder screaming at me. After a quick trip to the bathroom, I felt refreshed and ready.

I had spent the previous day driving from Toronto down to Pennsylvania, where I would spend three days on a writing retreat. I'd invited a few friends who were also writers, and we were going to hunker down and write for 24 hours straight.

Once everyone arrived, our hostess brought us dinner - a delicious spread of pasta, bread, and vegetables. We ate in silence, each of us lost in our own thoughts about what we wanted to create the next day.

The first morning started early, with a 5:30 am wake-up call (note to self: ask for room service instead). We wrote until lunchtime, then took a break outside to enjoy some sunshine. The fresh air and warm sun on my skin was just what I needed - it felt like I'd been cooped up in the hotel room forever.

One of the best parts about this retreat was that we didn't have access to phones or laptops during our writing time. We were forced to focus entirely on our work, which made for a really productive and focused 24 hours. Well, mostly focused - there was some gossiping going on over lunch (I won't name names), but overall the energy felt really positive.

One of my favorite parts about this experience was hearing what everyone else had written that day. It's always inspiring to read someone else's work, especially when you've been cooped up in a hotel room all morning and it feels like the sun isn't shining (which I know is crazy - it was actually shining pretty brightly).

We talked about our projects, and some of us even shared what we had written. It was such a supportive environment that I felt totally comfortable sharing my work. It's always scary to share your writing with others, but in this case it really felt like we were all in the same boat.

Another great part about this retreat was just how much food there was. The hostess brought us dinner again on the second night - a massive spread of pasta, meatballs, and chicken parmesan. We ate until we were stuffed, then sat around chatting for hours.

I think one of my favorite moments from the entire trip was when I realized that I had written over 4,000 words in just two days. That's a lot, right? It felt like a huge accomplishment to me - especially since I've been working on this book project for a while now. There were some other great moments too, but that one really stood out.

Now, as I sit at home and think about what happened over the past few days, I can see just how much I needed that break. Sometimes it takes getting away from all the distractions in our daily lives to do something creative and focus on our work. It doesn't have to be a three-day writing retreat (or even 24 hours) - sometimes even an hour or two of focused time is enough.

The most important thing for me was being able to write without distraction, which felt amazing after the past few weeks. I know this might sound weird, but it's actually really helpful when you can get away from all the things that could potentially distract you and just sit down with your writing. It feels like a big weight has been lifted off my shoulders - especially since I've been working on some pretty big projects.

I think one of the best ways to do this is by setting boundaries for yourself around how much time you spend focused on your work. This might mean telling people that you don't have time to chat or meet up, and sticking to your schedule (or not). It might also be about finding a quiet place where you can focus - like my hotel room in Pennsylvania.

The more I learn about writing the more I realize how much support we need as writers. We're often so isolated in our own heads that it's hard to know if we're on the right track or completely off base (or even worse, stuck). But having a few people around who can offer guidance and encouragement - like my writing friends at the retreat - is absolutely priceless.

The last day of the retreat flew by in an instant. We wrote some more, then packed up our things to head home. I was sad to leave because it felt so good to be there with these women and just focus on our work. But as soon as we got back into our cars and headed north, I knew that I would be back for another visit.

I stopped at a local diner in Ohio to grab some breakfast (and a much-needed coffee) before driving home to Toronto. They had the best pancakes - big, fluffy, and dripping with syrup. I was so hungry after not eating since lunch the day before that I practically devoured the entire short stack.

The drive back felt long, but it wasn't until I pulled into my driveway that I realized how much work I still had to do on this book project of mine. It's a big job - almost 50,000 words! But after getting away from all the distractions and focusing for three days straight, I felt confident that I could get it done.

I know I have a long way to go (and probably many more 3:00 am wake-up calls), but this experience reminded me why I love writing so much. It's not just about producing words on a page - it's about connection and community. When you're surrounded by people who understand the struggle of getting your work out there, you feel supported. You know that you're not alone in what you're trying to do.

I also realized how important it is for me to keep writing (and sharing) my stories. It's a way for me to process and make sense of the world around me. And when I'm doing something creative - like writing or painting - I feel more grounded, calm, and at peace.

It was really special to be able to share this experience with other women who are also passionate about their work. We can learn so much from each other and just by being together (in person), we're supporting each other in a way that feels authentic and meaningful.

I think I'm going to remember this trip for a long time - especially the feeling of having written over 4,000 words in two days. It's such an amazing feeling when you sit back and realize how much work you've accomplished in just 24 hours (or even less).

As we sat around chatting at dinner on our last night there, I felt grateful for this experience. It was a reminder that it's always okay to ask for support - especially from people who understand your work. And the more I think about it, the more I realize how important it is to keep taking breaks and focusing on my writing projects.

It wasn't until we were getting ready to leave that one of my friends asked me if I felt like I'd accomplished everything I wanted to in such a short amount of time. My response was an immediate yes - I knew right away that I had done all the work that I needed to do and then some.

The next morning, as I prepared to head home, I realized just how much I loved writing this book. It's not just about getting words on a page or telling a story (or even making money). It's also about connection - and when you connect with others around shared passions, you feel supported in ways that are hard to explain.

I spent the last few hours of our trip driving north through Pennsylvania and then into Ohio. The drive felt long, but it was an important reminder for me about just how much work I still had ahead of me on this book project (and probably many other things too).

baseline.response:
A week in a tiny cabin, surrounded by towering pines and a stream that babied me with gentle lapping sounds - what if you just let go of the constant need for productivity and allowed yourself to simply be? I spent those five blissful days doing just that.

I'd been itching to get out of my cozy but overwhelming home office, where the temptation to do everything at once always threatens to consume me. So when a friend offered her family's lake house rental for a week, I jumped at the chance. It was exactly what I needed - a break from the constant need to create and produce.

The cabin itself was tiny, with creaky wooden floors and a kitchen that seemed to be begging for a good scrubbing. But it had everything I needed: a writing nook, a comfy reading chair, and a view of the lake that would put even the most seasoned meditator to sleep. And then there were the surroundings - nature in all its untamed glory.

I spent my mornings sipping coffee on the porch, watching the mist clear off the water as the sun rose over the hills. Those quiet moments felt like a reset button for my entire being. I'd take a deep breath, feeling the stillness seep into my bones, and let my thoughts wander to whatever writing project needed attention.

And then - the best part - I started writing. Not forcing it, not trying to hit any word count targets, just allowing myself to flow onto the page like water over rocks. It was as if all the noise in my head had been muffled, leaving only this gentle hum of creativity that guided me along.

Afternoons were for experimenting with new recipes - think: wine-infused pasta sauce, blackberry jam on toast, and the occasional (okay, frequent) glass of Pinot Noir to keep things interesting. Dinner was often a solo affair, but I didn't mind; I'd sit by the fire, watching the stars twinkle to life above, feeling grateful for this time to recharge.

The best part? No social media, no email, no phone notifications screaming at me to "get back on track." Just me, my notebook, and the gentle lapping of that stream. Sometimes it's not about what you produce, but how you feel while producing it - or, in some cases, not producing anything at all.

So here's the question: What if you let go of your next big project for just one week? What if you surrendered to the idea that sometimes it's okay to do nothing at all?

examples.prompt: Write a blog post titled "Jacqueline Fisch - Wishbeads Guest Blog post"
examples.reference:
I've been a long-time fan of Wishbeads bracelets. First, and selfishly, for myself - I mean, who wouldn't want another tool to help make their wishes come true?

And then, as a writer and writing coach, I wanted to keep finding ways to surprise and delight my clients. I mean, I can only send people so many pens and notebooks and what better way to celebrate the work my clients accomplish than to support them in making their wishes come true.

What's even more amazing is hearing the stories and seeing the pictures of the beads on people's social media feed. My clients often become my friends, and it's incredibly satisfying to deepen a friendship by honoring each other's wishes. I keep a few dozen Wishbeads in a variety of colors stashed under my desk to surprise and delight my clients.

As I package them up, gratitude and a knowing smile always wash over me as I imagine the look on their face, and then them making a wish, rolling it up, and slipping it inside the secret compartment. And of course, when their dreams become a reality.

Swapping out my wishpaper when my wish has come true brings me tremendous joy.

I have a few Wishbeads bracelets, and they all contain a different wish. There's something that happens when you intentionally focus on a specific outcome - you notice all the ways your wish is already on its way to you.

What's also beautiful is how your wish might show up in a slightly different form. It's an excellent reminder to let go of the how. I realized along this wishing journey, that the more I pay attention and look for signs of my wish out there, the faster wishes come true. There's actually some brain science to this too - it's called the reticular activating system (or the RAS) and our brain shows us what we're focusing on. Same reason that once you decide to go Jeep shopping you can't help but notice all the Jeeps on the road.

This past summer, I wanted to give the ladies in my mastermind group a special gift - something we could all connect with, and deepen our relationship. Having shared many hours of conversations, connections, tears, and advice with them over the past year, I wanted to gift them something meaningful.

I'm also unreasonably practical, so the idea of giving a gift that sits on a shelf or doesn't get used hurts my heart a little.

Inside our mastermind group, we all have big goals and important dreams.

What if we all had Wishbeads to cement our highest hopes for ourselves and our businesses?

Wanting to give each of these important women a color they'd love, I sent everyone a cryptic message the evening before my flight to meet them at our annual retreat, "Black, blue, or green?" The three colors of bracelets I had beneath my desk. I collected their responses, tucked their Wishbeads into my carry-on bag and boarded the plane.

Our theme for our most recent group mastermind retreat was rejuvenation, and we had just spent a delicious hour vibrating along with a sound bath experience. It was my first sound bath experience - you lay down on yoga mats while someone creates sounds using a variety of instruments, including tuning forks, gongs, crystal singing bowls, chimes, and voice.

The sound bath left us deeply relaxed, so now was the perfect time to present the gifts.

I called each member of our group over and presented a Wishbeads bracelet in their chosen color, which was followed by gasps of delight, gratitude, and hugs. Having been an experienced "wisher," I offered up some suggestions on the specific language to use when writing their wishes.

The results of our group all leaving that evening, making wishes, and seeing them wearing their intentions was potent.

Some ladies shared their wishes later with the group, and some kept them private.

Within a few weeks the updates started rolling in:

My wish came true already!

I crushed it! Time for a new wish!

My wish is coming true!

It ended up looking slightly different, but still amazing!

Wishing as a group was a powerful and moving experience. In our inner circle, we held each other accountable and were able to hold the space, and in a way, amplify one another's wishes. Having insight into each other's big intentions also helped us to serve as reminders for one another - for those of us who shared our wish, we now had five other women keeping their eyes and ears open for clues.

Wishing alone can take you far, wishing together can take you farther!

Jacqueline Fisch is an intuitive writer, strategist, and coach for business owners who want to sound human while they make an impact in the world. She's the creator of Write Like a MOFO, an online writing community for writers, coaches, and business owners. In this podcast, we chat about the power of setting intentions and goals and then doing some dang work to get it done! Learn more about Jacq here.

**How to Keep Writing Through the Holidays: 7 Tips from a Seasoned Writer**

I've been writing for over a decade, but I still find that the holidays can throw me off my game. It's easy to get sidetracked by holiday baking (which is not as fun when you're eating all of your own creations) and family time.

Here are some ways I stay on track with my writing during the holidays:

**1. Set a goal**

My goal for this season was to write 5,000 words. This might seem like a lot if you're looking at it from an external perspective, but it's actually a manageable amount of words that will help me make progress on my larger projects.

**2. Plan ahead**

I started planning my holiday content well in advance - sometimes up to 3 months before the season starts. Having a plan helps me stay focused and makes sure I'm not scrambling at the last minute for ideas.

**3. Use your downtime wisely**

Instead of spending all day watching holiday movies, use some of that time to write. You can get more done in an hour than you might think, especially if you have a plan.

**4. Keep it short and sweet**

Don't feel like writing a 2,000-word blog post? Write a series of shorter posts instead! This will help you stay focused and make progress on your goals without feeling overwhelmed by a big piece of content.

**5. Make the most of morning pages**

Morning pages are a great way to get some writing done before anyone else wakes up in the house. I also use them as a place to brainstorm new ideas or work through any creative blocks.

**6. Keep your eyes on the prize**

Remember why you're doing this - because you love it, and because you have something valuable to say. Don't let others make you feel like you should be spending more time baking cookies than writing (as much as I adore cookies).

**7. Be intentional with your time**

If someone asks if they can schedule a meeting or call with you during the holidays, ask them when would work best for you - and then stick to that date and time.

As a writer, it's easy to get sucked into the idea of taking a break from writing during the holiday season. But by being intentional about your time and setting small goals, you can keep making progress on your projects even while surrounded by festive lights and family members who are eager to know what you're doing in there with all those words.

If you have any favorite ways to stay motivated and make progress on your writing during the holidays, I'd love to hear them. Let me know in the comments below!

Jacqueline Fisch is a writer, copywriter, and writing coach who helps women get their creative projects off the ground. She's been featured on The Write Life and has contributed to various publications including The Creative Penn.

Find more writing tips and inspiration from Jacqueline at How Women Write - a podcast dedicated to exploring what it means to be a female writer in today's world.

baseline.response:
**How to Break Free from Writing Shame (and Why You Should)**

I'm sitting here with my morning coffee, surrounded by the chaos of family life – aka my two tiny humans and their dad trying to cook breakfast while I attempt to write. It's a scene you might see in any given household, but it's also a reminder that I don't have to be writing like this all the time.

You know what holds us back from our best writing lives? Writing shame. Yes, that's a thing – and it's a real pain in the butt. It's the voice in your head telling you, "You're not good enough," or "No one wants to read your stuff." Sound familiar?

I used to be a corporate writer. The kind who cranked out reports with ease, but also felt like I was slowly dying inside. When I made the leap to full-time writing, it wasn't just about trading in my suit for yoga pants – it was about confronting this deep-seated shame.

Here's the thing: writing is not a competition. It's not a space where we try to outdo each other or prove our worth. It's about sharing our stories, our thoughts, and our experiences with others. So why do we let shame hold us back?

Just that. I mean, think about it – if you're holding onto writing shame, what are you really saying? That your words don't matter? That no one wants to hear from you?

Every single time I've tried to push through this shame and just write anyway, I've ended up with something beautiful. Not because I'm a "good" writer (I'm still working on that), but because I was willing to take the risk.

So what if you just... stopped trying to fix it? What if you let go of the need for perfection and just wrote from the heart?

Here's my promise to you: your words do matter. They may not change the world overnight, but they can spark something in someone else. And that's worth fighting for.

And – I know this might sound weird – but I'm going to give you a gift. It's called "write-ability." Yeah, I just made that up. But it's real. When we write from a place of shame, our words are laced with doubt and fear. We're not being true to ourselves.

So let's try something new. Let's take a deep breath, grab a cup of coffee (or tea, or wine – whatever fuels you), and just... write.

Not for anyone else, but for yourself. For the sake of putting words down on paper and seeing where they take us.

And if it's all crap? Well, that's okay too. Because sometimes we have to get the crap out before we can find what's truly beautiful.

Now, go ahead – I dare you. Write like no one is watching. Write with abandon. Write because you must.

Not even close to perfect yet, but getting there.

How will you break free from writing shame today?

examples.prompt: Describe the writing process as a journey of intentional motion and growth, where one's body of work is carefully curated to nourish and delight their audience.
examples.reference:
An object in motion tends to stay in motion. That's our goal here - motion. And not just any kind of motion: Intentional, grounded, and aligned motion to support your highest path. There will be bumps and it will be uncomfortable at times. The more you embrace discomfort and feel okay in it, the more you'll grow as a writer and as a person. Buckle up and enjoy the ride.

Chapter 20: Your body of work

In Chapter 13 we talked about your charcuterie board of content. If you're worried your content, books, and blog posts all read like a mishmash of topics, I invite you to think about everything you write about as a component that goes on one of these tastebud adventures on a wooden plank.

The tastes of the charcuterie board are delicious when enjoyed alone and even better when paired together. And with a delightfully curated presentation, it's also a feast for your eyes.

Applying the charcuterie concept to your writing, each of your blog posts, courses, and books are nourishing and helpful on its own. When enjoyed one after another with a small taste from all the other accompanying content - the experience is even better.

Your group programs paired with your weekly emails.

Your books paired with your newsletter.

Your social media captions paired with your poetry.

Your podcast paired with your workshops.

This is why organizing your content into buckets, or a handful of key themes, will help your work appear as a charcuterie board. You wouldn't drop mashed potatoes, Brussels sprouts, or spaghetti on your board. It wouldn't fit.

And now, if you're wondering how many themes you have or how even to begin organizing your body of work, here are some ways to figure them out.

Remember that each piece of writing you create is not for everyone. Think about the writers you enjoy - do you absolutely love every bit of their work? Maybe. But chances are that you enjoy some pieces more than others.

Just like the plant-based eater at your party, they're not going to be mad if there's prosciutto on your plate - they'll just eat the things they prefer.

1. Scan your existing writing.

Look across everything you've ever written. If you had to put all the topics into categories, where would they naturally fit? Then, consider your list of groups and think about combining or removing some if there are too many. Are there some topics you no longer write about? You can let those live on the internet and write about things you're most interested in now. If you notice you have a single theme, that's great too. You can absolutely stick to one subject or add more if you feel inspired in the months and years ahead.

2. Consider what you want to be known for.

How do you want people to remember you? Do you want them to remember you for your ability to tell a story and apply a life lesson? Do you want to be known for your no-BS talk? What subject matter do you want readers to associate with your name? Do you want to be a poet who cooks? A romance writer who's also a kickass marketer?

3. Consider what you could talk about all day long.

When you talk about something, and it brings you an inner feeling of excitement and enthusiasm, that right there is magic - follow it. Remember, what feels good to you to create feels good to your readers. List out all the topics that make you feel antsy with anticipation. Subjects so good that you can't wait to share what you know.

4. What do people always ask for your advice on?

If people constantly ask you for cooking advice and you're a life coach, you may want to keep those topics to a minimum. When you notice you're giving the same advice repeatedly, you've got a gift right there. That's the magic sauce you can serve to your audience.

Intuitive Writing Tip

Uncouple the idea that everything you write needs to make money. Some writing can (and should) be for fun or for helping others. Let your art be art without putting pressure on it to do something for you - like make you famous or rake in a million bucks.

5. Write out your list of themes - maybe you have one, three, or even six.

Review each topic on the list. Do you enjoy and want to continue talking about these topics? If not, go ahead and strike some. Maybe what you remove from your blog content is what you'll share in your books. And if you do love these topics, now you have some boundaries to help you brainstorm.

fine_tuned.response:
As you sit down to write, imagine yourself stepping onto a path that winds through mountains. You're about to begin a journey that requires intention - not just every step you take, but every thought, feeling, and word.

Your goal isn't just to get from point A to B. It's to experience the journey itself. To pay attention to your surroundings, to notice the subtle shifts in light and shadow as the sun rises over the mountains, casting long shadows behind the trees.

You're not rushing through this mountain pass. You're taking time to breathe, to pause, and to look around. Every step you take is an intentional motion - a conscious movement towards clarity and purpose.

Your writing is your body of work. As you make progress on your journey, it's not just about the destination. It's about how you feel along the way. You're nourishing yourself with every word, every sentence, and every story. And as you move forward, you're creating a landscape that will eventually bring delight to your readers.

Imagine standing at the top of the mountain pass, looking out over a vast expanse of rolling hills and valleys below. That's where your writing takes shape. It's not something you force - it's something that unfolds naturally from within you.

Your body of work isn't just about what you write. It's about how it makes people feel when they read it. Is it inspiring? Uplifting? Provoking? Challenging?

You're not trying to force-feed your audience anything. You're simply being a vessel for the wisdom, experiences, and stories that want to come through you. Your writing is your offering - a gift to share with others.

As you continue on this journey, you'll notice changes within yourself. You might feel more grounded, more confident, or more clear about what matters most to you. This isn't just luck or accident. It's the result of intentional motion and growth.

You're not trying to change who you are. You're simply evolving into a more authentic version of yourself - one that shines brighter with every step you take. And as you continue along this path, your writing will naturally reflect those changes.

Your body of work is an extension of yourself - an expression of who you are and what you bring to the world through your words. It's not something external or separate from you; it's a reflection of the journey you're on and all that you've learned along the way.

So as you continue down this mountain path, remember that every step is intentional. Every word matters. And every story - no matter how big or small - has the power to impact someone's life in a meaningful way.

The question now becomes: What kind of body of work do you want to leave behind?

baseline.response:
The writing life - it's like planting a garden, isn't it? You sow seeds of thought, tend to them with attention, and watch them bloom into words that feed the soul.

For me, the writing process starts before dawn, when my mind is still quiet and my body is awake but not yet alert. I savor a cup of strong coffee, feel the weight of my pen in hand, and let the blank page stare back at me like an open door. And then... I start to move.

Not always with purpose, mind you. Sometimes I just put words on paper because it's what my fingers demand. Other times, I'm chasing a thread of thought that refuses to be silenced. Either way, the act of writing becomes a form of yoga - a practice of bending and releasing, of surrendering to the flow.

As the days turn into weeks, and the pages start to fill up with words, the motion begins to shift. It's no longer just about putting pen to paper; it's about cultivating a conversation with my audience. I think back on the women who've shared their stories with me through the podcast - how they weave together threads of personal experience, intuition, and creative passion.

And that's when it hits me: our bodies of work aren't just collections of words; they're living, breathing ecosystems that nourish and delight those who engage with them. It's not about churning out content for its own sake; it's about curating a collection of writing that is an extension of ourselves - our quirks, our strengths, our messiness.

So I slow down, take my time, and ask myself: what do I want to feed my audience? What stories need to be told, what wisdom shared, what laughter evoked? And as the words start to flow, I'm no longer just writing; I'm tending a garden of connection, where each piece of work is carefully planted to flourish.

The journey may twist and turn, but with every step forward, I become more attuned to my own voice, my own rhythm. The motion deepens, and the words start to take on a life of their own - as if they're not just coming from me, but through me. And that's when the magic happens: we create something that's greater than the sum of its parts - a tapestry of words that weave together our hearts, our passions, and our unique perspectives.

What if you just let your writing process unfold like this? What if you trusted the motion to guide you, instead of trying to control every step along the way?

Just start moving. See where the words take you.

examples.prompt: Describe the strategies you use to manage your thoughts and emotions when faced with negative self-talk or criticism from others.
examples.reference:
One of my favorites is the cancel process. While I'm usually thoughtful with my words, sometimes my mouth moves faster than my brain, and I say something stupid or out of line. Wait! I take that back! Well, I do the same with my thinking. When a thought creeps up that dulls my shine, I say something in my head like cancel, cancel, cancel, or delete, delete, delete, or reject and delete.

Then, I get to tell a better story.

You are in charge of your thoughts. You can change them whenever you like. Hear a load of crap in your ear? Stop that thought and tell yourself another one. You don't have to be all fairy dust about it and tell yourself that you're a sparkly rainbow of luminous delight. Use words you'd use with close friends, like "Hey, this is completely normal, do better next time."

I also have a thank bank that I deposit praise, kind emails, and kudos into regularly. I keep mine in a Google Doc and in a special email folder that I literally named "Thank Bank." Every time I get a gushing email, or a small note of thanks or praise, I take a copy and store it in my thank bank. On days when my thoughts are running wild, I make a mistake, or actually do suck at something, I read through my thank bank and feel so much better.

Beyond showering myself in gratitude, sharing gratitude outward gives me an instant boost. Sometimes I write a thank-you note on a card and drop it in the mail, send a nice email, or write a review of a favorite restaurant.

In addition, consider getting a coach, a therapist, and some friends who will check your limiting thoughts at the door. Even better, get all three - remember, you become the essence of the five people you spend the most time with.

For most of my life I tried to go it alone. The lone warrior, I always had an idea that asking for help made me weak. I'm still unraveling those behaviors. The more you ask for help, the more you realize that people want to help. Especially when those people are good at it.

Here's what my support network looks like these days - Ryan holding up the other end of our household in his career, striving for more and better, and pushing me to be better. My kids to show me where I need to work on myself, and for them to do chores around the house (for this reason, a housekeeper is not yet part of my support system). A paid mastermind because I don't want to be the smartest person in a room. Business besties - friends I can text in the middle of the day when I'm stuck or need to bounce my latest idea off someone. A coach - who's supported me in wiping out negative emotions (yeah, I didn't think it was possible either) and showing me how to train my brain to expect good things. My work with my coach has been a game changer. My family, who I can always count on. Even though we're five hundred miles away, they're my number one fans.

An objective opinion will be able to spot a negative thought spiral a mile away and can help you reframe it. And it takes practice. I've spent more than a decade consciously looking at my thoughts, and there's still some gunk that comes up in there from time to time. It's going to happen - I can use the opportunity to strengthen my mind, and over time, it becomes a habit.

You've got a masterpiece to create

When I was first thinking about starting a business - remember, eight whole years before I had enough momentum to take it full time, I spent months trying to figure it out by staring at my computer, filling out worksheets, and downloading every free PDF on the internet - I was doing a lot of things that made me feel like I was building something but didn't actually take me any further down the road. I was desperate to finally figure out what it was that I wanted to do with my life. It was as if someone else or something outside of me had all the answers.

Night after night, I'd eat an inappropriate amount of chocolate and drink just a little more wine than I'd planned. I'd have an idea - I'm going to only write about hummus! I wish I was joking. I thought I was going to build a business around dip. Now, I believe you can create a business around just about anything. Building a thriving income around dip might have been a stretch, though.

fine_tuned.response:
You're going to want to take a step back and ask yourself: What are you thinking? Who is telling you this? Is it true?

When I'm faced with negative self-talk or criticism from others, here's how I manage my thoughts and emotions:

First, I pause. I put down whatever I was doing and take a breath in through my nose and out through my mouth.

Then, I ask myself if what I'm thinking is really true. If I believe the negative narrative, it might be helpful to write about why that's the case. Is this story something I've been telling myself for years? Is it serving me?

Once I have some clarity around whether or not it's even a valid thing to think, I ask myself if I'm willing to let go of it. Am I ready to move on from this belief? If so, I make a conscious decision to release it.

If the negative self-talk is coming from someone else, I try to put myself in their shoes and see where they're coming from. What are they afraid of? Are they insecure about something?

Then, I look at my own strengths and accomplishments. How many times have you accomplished something amazing today? Last week? Last year? You've got this! Keep going.

And if all else fails, I remind myself that negative thoughts are just that - temporary visitors in my mind and heart. They don't define me or my worth. They're not facts; they're just thoughts.

Finally, I tell myself something kind, like: "I'm doing the best I can," or, "That's okay, it's only a mistake if you let it be."

So, let's talk about the inevitable - that inner critic who loves to show up uninvited. You know, the one who's always like, "You're not good enough," or "Your writing is crap." Yeah, I've got a few choice words for her, too.

First of all, I want you to try something with me: when those voices come up - and they will come up - just acknowledge them. Say it out loud, or to yourself: "Okay, inner critic, I see you. You're here again." Don't engage, don't argue, just observe.

Now, here's where things get interesting. Have you ever noticed how our brains love to catastrophize? Yeah, mine does too. So when that inner critic shows up, my brain starts racing with all the worst-case scenarios: "You'll never finish this project," or "Your writing will never be good enough." But I've learned a trick to shut that down.

I call it the "pause and pivot" technique. When those thoughts start spiraling out of control, I pause - just for a second. Literally, take a deep breath in, hold it for a beat, and exhale slowly. And then, pivot. Ask yourself: "What would happen if...?" What if this project does take longer than expected? What if my writing isn't perfect? (It won't be.)

You see, our brains are wired to seek perfection, but the truth is, it's just not possible. And what happens when we aim for perfection? We freeze up, we procrastinate, and we give in to that inner critic.

But here's the thing: you don't have to believe every negative thought that crosses your mind. You can choose to reframe them. So, when that voice says, "You're not good enough," I say back to it: "I'm doing my best with what I've got right now." And if someone else tells me I'm not good enough? Well, that's their problem, not mine.

One final thing to remember: you are not your thoughts. They're just passing through, like clouds in the sky. You get to choose how you respond to them. So, take a deep breath, and remind yourself: "I trust my process."

examples.reference:
I love a good writing prompt. Sometimes I look at writing prompts, especially when they're in a prescribed format when I have to follow them in order, and they feel stifling.

Or, I read a prompt, ready to dive into my unconscious mind to see what wants to come out, and it just feels off. I can't explain the feeling, but it doesn't feel exciting to write about.

A few years ago, I started aligning my content writing calendar with the astrological calendar.

So why not have a set of writing prompts to explore during every astrological season?

How to use these prompts:

Save this article to come back to throughout the year and visit each sun sign's writing prompts as we move from season to season.

Choose to ponder, write about, or explore one prompt a week for the season.

Have fun with them and come back to them.

You can use these to drive content inspiration for your business, or writing ideas for your blog and emails - just use them.

Play with the writing prompts for your sun, rising, and moon signs. Don't know what those are? Find out for free on astro.com with your date, time, and place of birth, or join my favorite astrologer's community - Luminary Parenting.

Aries: The Ram

Dates: Mar 21 - Apr 19

Mode + Element: Cardinal Fire

What lights your fire?

Write 5-10 "I am..." statements in positive toward language.

Hold a ceremony to initiate yourself as a writer.

Write out the details of your most abundant launch. How do people feel signing up? Where are you? How do you celebrate?

During what event did you feel truly fearless? Where is that energy needed today?

Taurus: The Bull

Dates: Apr 20 - May 20

Mode + Element: Fixed Earth

List 10 things you have - Can be material goods, strengths, or qualities that help you feel safe, secure, and at peace.

How can you serve your current customers ever more powerfully?

Review your body of work. Write gratitude for your favorite creations.

Put your bare feet on the ground and ask, who am I?

Review your list of unfinished projects. Choose one project and a target completion date. What resources do you need to finish it? Who can help? Write it on your calendar and plan your finishing strategy.

Gemini: The Twins

Dates: May 21 - Jun 20

Mode + Element: Mutable Air

What do you want to know from your audience? Send an email and ask them.

Write your argument for what you believe. Now get curious, and write the opposite.

What difficult conversation do you need to have? Write what you'll say in an email or letter. Only send it if you want to.

Describe the way you'd like to improve your communication.

Describe your favorite co-creation with the Divine.

Cancer: The Crab

Dates: June 21 - July 22

Mode + Element: Cardinal Water

Write about how you feel around bodies of water.

Where can you nurture your inner writer more deeply?

How do your clients like to be nurtured?

Go above and beyond to nurture your next prospective client. What do you do?

Create a survey for your customers with all the things you'd love to know but were afraid to ask.

Reconnect with a past customer or your email list.

Leo: The Lion

Dates: July 23 - Aug 22

Declare what you'll do this month in a series of "I will" statements.

Where do you crave more visibility? Where can you promote yourself?

Plan your next launch with all the bold details, including how you'll celebrate.

Commit a day this week to looking for sparks.

Plan the rest of your year.

Is your authentic self showing up in your writing?

Where are you called to go BIG?

Virgo: The Virgin

Dates: Aug 23 - Sep 22

Mode + Element: Mutable Earth

What's your favorite way to serve?

Ask your readers, "How can I best serve you today?"

Go through all your old notes and files. What feels valuable to pay more attention to?

How do you feel about your current content format (blogging, podcasting, social media posting)? What else could you explore?

List out your strengths in your teaching style.

Look at your well-being routine. Think about where it needs an upgrade.

Libra: The Scales

Dates: Sep 23 - Oct 22

Mode + Element: Cardinal Air

If you could collaborate with your favorite creative or business person, what would you make together?

How can you improve your relationship with your reader?

Where are you craving more balance?

Revisit your writing space and make some small upgrades to make it more inviting.

Craft a vision board of your dreamiest writing retreat.

List your most trusted business friends. Do they fit where you're going? Is there anyone missing?

Scorpio: The Scorpion

Dates: Oct 23 - Nov 21

Mode + Element: Fixed Water

Review the parts of your client's lives that you change and write 10 declarations starting with, "I transform..."

Share a case study of a client transformation.

Write about a transformation you've been through how you achieved it.

What work is calling for you to put your head down and go deep?

What will you commit to?

How could you put yourself out there (even if it feels scary) just to see what happens?

Sagittarius: The Archer

Dates: Nov 22 - Dec 21

Mode + Element: Mutable Fire

Think about your next writing project and what you see it doing for your reader. Commit these to a series of "I see" statements.

Allow yourself to dream big - what does it look like?

What wisdom do you love to share? How can you share more?

Where can you take three new risks in your business or writing?

Create your vision for the coming year. Then 5 years from now. Pretend it's here today.

Capricorn: The Goat

Dates: Dec 22 - Jan 19

Mode + Element: Cardinal Earth

Plan the next three months of your writing and map out a plan to get it done.

What are people always asking for your advice on? Write a how-to blog or email.

Create your (unfussy) content plan for the coming year.

Take your big dreams and create a to-do list. Then plot out when you'll take action on these items.

Aquarius: The Water Bearer

Dates: Jan 20 - Feb 18

Mode + Element: Fixed Air

What's my favorite invention?

Where would it be fun to push boundaries?

If I could write anything at all, what would that be?

Choose a completely unrelated industry and go exploring. You never knew what you might find that would relate to your business or writing project.

Look at your recent content. Did you ask readers to share? Add a CTA to your most recent articles and your next newsletter.

I know this to be true...

Pisces: The Fish

Dates: Feb 19 - Mar 20

Mode + Element: Mutable Water

I used to believe__________. And today I believe___________.

Search images of your favorite artist, make one piece your computer or phone's background.

Bring to mind the last time you were in the flow of writing. How did it feel? Where were you? What did you create?

Where does your inner writer need healing?

Plan your dream writing retreat.

The astrological transits are calling. You've been feeling the nudge to write, and your business is begging you to get it out there.

If you're feeling called to lead or guide others, what kind of energy do you need to bring in? How can you harness the power of Mars to fuel your fire and drive your passion projects forward?

What do you need to believe about yourself and your work?

How do you want to connect with others through your writing?

Consider the sign of your sun. What does it say about how you show up for yourself? How can you use that same energy to connect with others through your writing?

1. Choose a prompt above - any one will do!
2. Look at what signs are influencing your emotions, decisions, or creative process right now.
3. Ask yourself questions like, "What kind of energy does this sign bring?" or "How can I harness its power?"
4. Let the answers guide you as you write.
5. Share it with the world!

**Your Sun Sign's Secret**

**Moon Phases and Your Creative Cycles**

Do you know the different moon phases? I used to think they were just for... well, lunar enthusiasts. But trust me, as a writer and coach, understanding these cycles has helped me tap into my own creative rhythms.

examples.prompt: Write a blog post titled "What if it was easy"
examples.reference:
I've been thinking a lot lately about the create-on-demand process that's a huge part of my work.

My clients rely on me to pull some clear and crisp writing out of my head and get it onto the page. And there's almost always a deadline.

Even this blog for instance. I've been blogging consistently since I don't remember when. Usually, subscribers get a weekly email from me on Tuesday, occasionally Wednesday.

And this week, the topic is also what I most needed to hear.

What if it was easy?

Have you ever been in the flow of writing and it just felt easy, natural, effortless. Dare I say, you were even having fun?

Maybe you weren't having any kind of feelings at all. You just sat down and started writing, and it came out of you.

Your energy was in the right place and it just fell out. Like it was waiting right there on your fingertips for the right opportunity for you to get out of your head for five minutes and just write.

What if writing was easy?

What would your blog look like if blogging was easy?

Would you sit down to write each week and just let the words flow out of you? Would you go back and forth 90 billion times about what topic to write about?

Or, could it just be easy?

What would your newsletter look like if it was easy?

What would a batch writing session look like if it was easy?

What would you be drinking?

What will you wear?

Where will you sit?

What's your posture like?

What's playing through your speakers?

What essential oil is being infused into the air?

What does your face look like? Crunched up or relaxed?

What's all around you?

You see, when it's easy, it's faster. When it's easy, it feels good. When it's easy, it just works.

Easy get's noticed. Not because you took the direct path, but because your energy was there. The energy you brought to the page was clean and open and ready to spill over.

Your readers notice.

They notice the way you make them feel.

So why the hell shouldn't it be easy?

The next time you're stuck in a shit sandwich or staring down a task you just don't want to start before you begin, ask this question....

What would it look like if it was easy?

Begin there.

fine_tuned.response:
What if it was easy?

It's one of those questions that makes you pause.

When I first started my business, I thought being an entrepreneur would be hard. Like super-hard. It wasn't going to feel good. I'd work long hours, make lots of money, and people would love me for it. But the more I got into it, the more I realized how much time and energy I was putting in - only to make a fraction of what I wanted.

I felt like I was swimming upstream. And every step forward felt like two steps back.

What if it wasn't that hard?

What if instead of working 40+ hours a week for a salary, you could create a business that made money while you slept? That didn't require your constant attention and presence to be successful?

What if you could focus on the work that lights you up - the work that makes you feel good - without worrying about making enough money or not having time?

What if it was easy?

For me, what I realized after years of trial and error is that being an entrepreneur doesn't have to be hard. It can actually be quite fun.

And here's the thing: most people who aren't entrepreneurs don't understand how easy it can be. They think you need a fancy degree or experience in your industry. They think you need a big bank account and a full-time team of employees.

You don't.

All you really need is:

A clear idea of what you want to create

The willingness to take consistent action towards that goal

Trusting yourself (and the process) every step of the way

It's easy when you have a clear vision, know your worth, and are taking actions that align with who you are.

What if it was easy?

How would you feel? How would you show up in business? In life?

It starts with one question: what do I want to create?

From there, the answers start rolling in. The rest is just a matter of taking action - trusting yourself every step of the way.

Easy does it!

What if showing up at your desk every morning felt like a gentle stroll rather than a marathon? What if writing came as naturally as breathing, without all the inner monologues about "am I good enough?" or "will anyone even care?"

This isn't some pie-in-the-sky fantasy. It's real life – and it's within reach.

Here's what I know: when we let go of the need to force it, to will it into existence, that's exactly when the magic happens. When we trust our bodies and our minds enough to say, "you know what? Today feels like a writing day," then watch out.

Not perfect – but beautiful.

And that's what it's all about, isn't it? Beauty, not perfection.

So here's your assignment for today: take a deep breath and ask yourself – what if it was easy?

No pressure. No expectations. Just curiosity.

What if the writing just happened?

Sound familiar?

examples.prompt: Write a blog post titled "Finding your way"
examples.reference:
It was at least a year ago when you had the inkling to quit your day job and try something different. Whether you're doing the same job, except as a freelancer, or switching careers completely, you're getting close to quitting time.

I first got that little itch to start my own business in 2009 (eight years ago from this writing). My son was a year old, and I had just been laid off for the first time (turned out it would happen twice more). Except I had no ideas, so I got another job.

Again in 2011, while pregnant with my daughter, I came across a book on my lunch hour, called the Firestarter Sessions. It was all about finding work that you love. The kind of work you were meant to do.

Oh - this is a thing? I don't need to have a job? I can create work that's perfect for me? I had no idea.

I dug into those worksheets like my life depended on it. I wasn't sure what to do. I started a food and lifestyle blog.

Five years later, still brainstorming, still pouring over the worksheets, wondering what that work was that I was supposed to do, it finally hit me - I want to write. I want to write more for myself and others. I had written a book a few years prior; I wanted to do more. And I wanted to write for other people. I was doing in a corporate setting, but I needed more control over my time and the work I took on.

My point - keep trying different ideas. There's insight inside every action.

Here's a list of all the things I tried to turn into a business:

Food blogging

Cookbook writing

Cooking classes

Private cooking lessons

Customized meal plans based on people's taste buds

Minimalism coaching

Accountability coaching

If I didn't try all these things, I might not have landed on what I'm doing today. The side hustle that turned into a profitable business and replaced my corporate income might have never happened, and I'd still be commuting three hours a day and rushing around like a frazzled mom trying to keep everything together.

If you're sitting there reading this and are thinking that you want to do something "else," and you just don't know what that something else is just yet, just keep looking.

Reading some self-help books and doing some workbooks can help, but there are only so many online courses and experts you can listen to. You need to listen to the most important expert - you.

Try one thing. Do it until it doesn't work anymore, or make tweaks and keep plugging on. Keep doing that until you find work that feels good.

Get out of your head and off the couch, and out from behind the laptop. The only way you'll know is if you take action and give things a try. Hey, you might land on something that really lights your fire right away, or maybe it will take 30 tries. Maybe you'll never land on one thing; you'll land on doing a few things.

fine_tuned.response:
Finding Your Way

You're in a season of discovery, and you don't know what to do. You have a feeling that there's more for you than the life you're living right now - but how do you figure out what that is?

The problem is that finding your way can be scary. It requires stepping outside of your comfort zone and into uncertainty. And if you've been in your head, overthinking things, then I totally get it.

I have a friend who's a great example of this. She was living the life she thought she should live - working a job, having two kids, being married to her high school sweetheart - but something felt off. Every time she'd talk about her "day," she'd say things like, "I'm so exhausted" and "Ugh, I hate my job."

One day, she had a conversation with her husband that went like this:

"I don't know what's wrong with me. I feel empty all the time. What am I even doing here?"

She didn't have an answer - but she knew something needed to change.

It took her some time, but eventually, she started making changes. She started taking classes online (which turned into a full-time business), and then she decided to leave her job and start working from home.

Now, it wasn't easy for her. There were days when she felt like hiding under the covers and not talking to anyone because she was so anxious about what other people would think of her new path. But here's the thing: she didn't let fear stop her.

She kept moving forward - one small step at a time, taking tiny actions that felt safe for her. And over time, something magical happened.

Her business started growing. She found more purpose and meaning in her work. And she had more energy than ever before (she even started doing yoga again!).

Now, I know what you're thinking: "But Jacq, this is all so scary. What if it doesn't work out?"

Here's the thing: You don't have to have all the answers right now. In fact, you'll never have them all figured out - that's just not how life works.

What you do need to know is this:

You're capable of finding your way

And I believe you because you've been finding your way already. Maybe it hasn't always felt like it, but you've been making decisions and taking actions every single day. And if you were completely lost - then you wouldn't be here today.

So take a deep breath and trust that you're on the right path for you.

What's holding you back from finding your way?

You might feel stuck in your current situation, or perhaps you feel like you don't even know what you want. The good news is that you can figure it out - but first, you have to let go of what you think you should be doing and start listening to what you actually want.

Here's a suggestion: take some time this week to write about what's not working for you in your current life. What are the things that feel forced or empty? Then, ask yourself these questions:

What do I love doing?

Where am I feeling stuck?

Who can I talk to about my dreams and desires?

And most importantly - what small step can I take today to move closer to where I want to be?

You don't have to have all the answers right now. Just start by taking one small action, and see where it takes you.

And if you need some support along the way, come join me in my writing community - How Women Write. We're a safe space for women who are on a journey of discovery - and we're here to help you find your way too!

baseline.response:
The perpetual question: where do I start? You've been staring at that blank page for what feels like an eternity, wondering how to coax those words out. And honestly, it's not about finding the perfect starting point – it's about showing up.

I used to be a total planner. I'd spend hours pouring over my paper day planner, color-coding and making sure every minute was accounted for. But let me tell you, that didn't exactly translate to creativity. In fact, it usually left me feeling like I was suffocating under the weight of my own expectations.

It wasn't until I started writing from a place of intuition – following my gut, listening to my inner voice – that things really shifted. It's not about having a clear plan or knowing exactly what you're doing; it's about trusting the process.

You know how sometimes you're on a road trip and you don't even realize where you are until someone points out the sign? That's kinda like writing. You'll be cruising along, tapping away at your keyboard, when suddenly – BAM! – something amazing happens. A character reveals themselves, or a plot twist emerges from nowhere. It's those moments that remind us why we started writing in the first place.

So here's the thing: you don't have to know what you're doing. You just have to show up. And trust me, it's scary as hell. But also kind of liberating. I mean, who needs a five-year plan when you can write with reckless abandon?

You are not alone in this struggle. Every writer – yes, even the ones who seem like they've got their shit together – has been there. Elizabeth Gilbert talks about this in Big Magic; the idea that creativity is a muscle we must exercise regularly, lest it atrophy from disuse.

So what if you just... stopped trying to fix it? What if you let go of the need for perfection and simply wrote?

Every single time I've tried to force my writing into some semblance of order, I've ended up with a piece that's as dry as the Sahara desert. But when I surrender – when I let myself meander through the process without an agenda – that's when the magic happens.

Not even close.

examples.prompt: Write about why women should stop waiting for permission to write
examples.reference:
Here's what nobody tells you about writing — you don't need permission.

Not from your partner. Not from your kids. Not from that voice in your head that says you should be doing laundry instead.

You just need to sit down and do it.

I know, I know. Easier said than done. But here's the thing — every woman I've coached, every single one, has said some version of "I'll start writing when..." When the kids are older. When work slows down. When I have something worth saying.

Screw that.

You have something worth saying right now. Today. This minute.

And I'm not talking about some grand literary masterpiece. I'm talking about the stuff that's been rattling around in your head at 2am. The stories you tell your best friend over wine. The observations that make you stop mid-walk and think — damn, that's something.

That's your writing. It already exists.

So why are you waiting? Who exactly is supposed to hand you a golden ticket that says "You May Now Write"? Because I'll tell you — that person doesn't exist. She never did.

The permission slip is yours to write. Always has been.

Start messy. Start scared. Start with ten minutes before the house wakes up.

Just start.

fine_tuned.response:
You're just going to sit there and wait for someone to tell you it's okay to start writing? It's okay to start showing up on the page, to let your words out into the world?

No one is going to give you permission. You have to be willing to take that leap yourself.

Maybe you've been waiting because you feel like you're not good enough yet. Maybe you're worried about what others will think of your writing or if it's "good" enough for publication, or if anyone even cares.

I know this feeling all too well. I was that girl who waited for permission to write for years - permission from a publisher, an editor, a mentor, or even just my family members.

But here's the thing: you're not waiting for permission because of what others think; you're doing it because you want to believe your words are important enough for others to read. You're holding yourself back because you don't trust that you have something valuable to share.

That's not a healthy or helpful way to write, and it's certainly not how women need to show up on the page. We've been silenced long enough; we don't need permission from anyone else to speak our truth.

And for goodness' sake - we're not going to wait for someone to give us permission to share what needs to be said in this world. The world is too busy, and it's too late already. It's time to trust your words are important, and that you have something valuable to say.

You've been waiting long enough; now it's time to start writing.

How do I know this? Because I did it myself - and I'm still doing it today. With every sentence, with every word, with every blog post or email newsletter, I'm showing up on the page and speaking my truth. And you can too.

It's okay if your words aren't perfect; they never will be anyway. It's not about making sure everyone likes what we have to say; it's about sharing what needs to be said - no matter how messy or imperfect that may look.

You're going to write until you run out of breath, or your fingers are bleeding, or you fall asleep with a pen in hand. And then you'll get up and do it all again tomorrow, because every time you show up on the page, something beautiful happens. Something raw and real and honest - just like you.

So stop waiting for permission to write; start writing anyway.

So you're waiting. Waiting for someone, anyone, to give you the green light to put pen to paper. Newsflash: no one's gonna show up at your doorstep with a badge and a clipboard saying "You're good to go, girl!" And even if they did, would that really be what you want? To have some outside force validating your creativity?

Think about it: how many hours have we spent in meetings, conferences, or workshops, listening to well-meaning women tell us to "trust our voice" and "follow our passion"? Meanwhile, back at home, the inner critic is still whispering, "But what if you're not good enough?" "What if nobody likes it?"

It's like we're waiting for some magical permission slip to show up in the mail. And until that day comes (spoiler: it never will), we'll be stuck in this limbo of self-doubt and fear.

Here's a little secret, though: writing is not about permission. It's about showing up. Every. Single. Day. Without expectation, without apology, without waiting for someone else to tell us it's okay.

And I know what you're thinking: "But Jacq, this sounds like just another pie-in-the-sky inspirational piece." Maybe it is. But hear me out. This isn't about being a "real writer" or producing some bestselling novel. It's about trusting that your words matter, even if nobody else does.

When I was still working in corporate, I used to think that writing was this huge, daunting task. Like, what if nobody likes it? What if I'm not good enough? But then I started writing for myself – no deadlines, no audiences, just me and the page.

And you know what happened? My words started flowing. And with them came a sense of freedom, of release, that I'd never felt before. I was writing because I had to, not because some editor or agent or friend told me to.

So let's make a pact right now: no more waiting for permission. No more second-guessing ourselves. We'll just show up – with our messy thoughts, our doubts, and our fears. And maybe, just maybe, that's when the real magic starts happening.

What if you stopped waiting for permission to write? What would happen next?

examples.prompt: Write a blog post about crisis comms
examples.reference:
## things I learned from working in crisis communications for three years

From 2009 until 2012 I worked for a major telecommunications company. BlackBerry, maybe you've heard of them? Back then, they were called RIM. Yes, I had RIM-job (had to).

Even though most of us are wielding iPhones now... you know from the other fruit company (not me though, Android all the way because Apple was the enemy back in those BlueBerry days.

Back in 2009 though, BlackBerry was in its heyday. My first job interviews them was the day after their private Van Halen concert. Well, shit, this company sounds pretty cool.

I landed my RIM-job shortly after getting laid off for the first time from a Chicago-based management consulting company. I am grateful for everything the company did... like relocated our family of three at the time from Chicago to the Greater Toronto Area and made sure everything was smooth.

This was my first formal communications job. Sure, I had written tons up until this point. Proposals worth millions of dollars, emails worth even more, but they had rules and process, and it was there that I really started paying attention to word choice.

The experiences I had at BlackBerry serve me today. When you mess up and send the wrong thing to the wrong client when poo flies sideways, and you need to smooth it over.

While I was working there, anytime the BlackBerry service would go down, for any wireless carrier in the world; it was my job to talk to the engineers, find out what happened, and turn around a perfectly polished statement. It had to happen usually within a day, and if it didn't, it could cost the company money.

Here are ## key lessons that could serve you in your business:

Use the language of possibility. For example, instead of saying, "Customers couldn't surf the internet." You'd say, "Customer might not have been able to use the internet." Do you see the difference?

Focus on the positive. What did you learn from screwing up? In systems speak, we'd say something like, "Now that we've identified this, we're working to add additional system components to make it stronger to avoid it happening in the future."

Don't use the F-word. That would be failure. Ack! How dirty is the word failure? Use this word enough, such as calling yourself the F-word, or saying you feel like one. Keep saying that out loud enough, and people will start to believe it.

Keep the blame. Sometimes BlackBerry service would go down, but it was a problem on a carrier's end. We wouldn't say, "It's your fault bucko - piss off." Incorporating language of possibility and keeping it positive, we'd say something like, "Users might have experienced problem using this feature for 10 minutes and we haven't determined the source of the problem."

When you don't know something, say so. Never make something up. It's okay just to say you don't know.

Be specific. Details matter. Don't say, "I'm a screw-up" say, "I screwed that response up." Put the focus on the thing, NOT on you.

Don't apologize. This is a tricky one, and if you've truly messed up, don't be an asshat - just say, "I'm sorry." Instead, turn it around to be positive. Like, if you missed a deadline or something is taking longer to deliver than you promised, or you've taken two weeks to answer an email, try, "Thank you for your patience." See the difference? Repeat after me, "Thank. You. For. Your. Patience." Instead of placing focus on the F-up that you made, focus on them while you're complimenting them for their patients.

Keep it short. Brevity is the key to my heart. When it comes to communicating a crisis, if you drone on and on, you're just going to make people angrier. Keep it simple, edit out extra words ruthlessly.

Proofread, proofread, proofread. When you're communicating a craptastic situation, it needs to be correct. I like to call this flawless execution. It's one of the handful of times in my life being a perfectionist really serves me well. Print it out, have a friend review it. At BlackBerry, at least five people touched each statement. An engineer to make sure we weren't talking out of our ass and to make sure we weren't sharing proprietary information, a peer, and two senior folks. It had to be on time, and it had to be right.

Words always matter. They matter even more when poo is on the ceiling. Take

fine_tuned.response:
5 Crisis Communication Tips to Help You Hold Your Ground (And Save Your Reputation)

As someone who has been in more than one shit storm, I can tell you that when it comes to crisis communications - your response matters.

There are two ways to handle a crisis. The first is to hide under the bed and hope it goes away. The second is to stand up straight, look at what's happening, say something about it, and do what needs to be done to fix it.

And if you're wondering why crisis communications isn't just a good idea - it's not - then ask yourself this: What if your brand or business can't get out of the news cycle? What if people keep talking about how bad you've been? What if you lose customers, and worse, employees?

Here are five tips to help you handle a crisis.

When shit hits the fan (and it will), say something right away. Don't wait for your team to come up with a plan - that takes time. You need to be out in front of this thing as soon as possible. It doesn't matter if it's not perfect, or even if you're not sure what happened.

Just get ahead of the story and start talking about it. I don't care if your team is still scrambling to figure out what went wrong - you can (and should) talk. Say something like, "We're aware there was an issue with [thing] and we're investigating/looking into it."

You might think that saying something before having all the facts will hurt your reputation more than good old silence. But if you keep quiet, you'll look like a bad actor trying to hide something.

If you don't say anything - at least not initially - people are going to fill in the blanks for you. And they won't be kind or accurate.

This is what happened during the 2013 Black Friday shopping mall shooting incident in New Jersey. A mother of two shot and killed three people, then herself. The police were slow to respond to questions about why there was a delay in how long it took them to arrive on the scene (it took around 20 minutes).

It wasn't until days later that they finally addressed questions from reporters and the public about what happened. And when they did, their responses came across as evasive - "We're looking into this." "The investigation is ongoing."

If you don't speak up immediately after a crisis, it looks like you have something to hide.

It's also important to be transparent in your communications during a crisis. This means giving people the facts and being honest about what happened, even if it hurts - especially when those facts are negative.

One example of this was how General Motors handled the ignition switch recall in 2014. The company had been aware of the problem since at least 2005 but didn't do anything about it until a couple of years later, after multiple deaths and injuries were reported.

When the crisis hit the fan, GM issued a statement saying that they were "sorry" for what happened - but also said that they would cooperate fully with any investigation. They didn't try to hide behind corporate speak or spin their response.

Instead, they immediately admitted fault and apologized for their actions.

It's always better to be honest in a crisis than to deny it altogether.

4. Don't apologize if you don't need to.

Apologies can be helpful during a crisis - but only when they're genuine. If you say sorry just to placate people or make them go away, then that can come across as insincere and even worse.

When there's been an actual mistake on your part (like ignoring something important), an apology is in order. But if it wasn't really your fault, don't apologize for something that isn't yours to own.

You could say something like this:

"I understand why you're upset. I'm upset too. This was a mistake and we should have done things differently."

This acknowledges the situation without going overboard with an apology.

5. Use it as an opportunity to shine.

A crisis is not only an opportunity to communicate what happened but also to show how you handle difficult situations - especially if there's been a mistake or failure on your part.

It's a chance for you to step up and take control of the situation. You can use the crisis as an opportunity to demonstrate leadership, integrity, and accountability.

When General Motors recalled their cars due to ignition switch problems in 2014, they used it as an opportunity to show that they were committed to safety - even if it meant making a difficult decision. They said something like:

"We've made a commitment to our customers and employees that we will do everything possible to ensure this doesn't happen again."

This message came across as one of regret for what happened but also a promise to learn from the mistake.

It's always easier (and less painful) to communicate during a crisis than it is after you've had time to think about how to respond. When something happens, take a deep breath and speak up immediately.
